caveats: a: The occupational mix is 2016 and four-digit rather than current six-digit data.
a: The machine-learning case is from flat-glass furnace control and only an adjacent process proxy.
rho: No representative 327213 implementation or realized-savings dataset was found.
rho: This is the implementation share of exposed opportunity, not a prediction for total productivity.
e: The injected n of 11 is an ESTIMATE derived from receipts and a sector margin rather than observed 327213 EBITDA.
e: Firm and establishment boundaries can differ substantially in a multi-plant manufacturing industry.
s5: Neither the eligible denominator nor qualifying transfer numerator is observed.
s5: A single transaction materially changes the measured rate when the population is this small.
q: No representative contract-level pass-through data was available.
q: Retention can differ sharply between specialized pharmaceutical or cosmetic containers and high-volume beverage bottles.
d5: Production is not demand and can move with imports, exports, inventories, and plant closures.
d5: EPA's material data are from 2018 and document the installed container stream, not a current growth forecast.
o: This is bounded judgment rather than a measured share.
o: Extensive automation can reduce staffing without eliminating the accountable operating firm under this definition.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Repeat business-to-business container sales under volume programs, forecasts, purchase orders, or supply contracts, priced primarily by unit and specification with energy, freight, and raw-material economics influencing negotiations.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive internal plants, non-control investment vehicles, pure distributors, container fillers, cullet processors that do not manufacture containers, and manufacturers of non-container glass products are excluded.
lens.included_activities: U.S. lower-middle-market manufacturers of glass packaging, bottling, and canning containers that repeatedly supply external beverage, food, pharmaceutical, cosmetic, or other packaging customers.

a.bridge: BLS provides employment and wages for four-digit 327200, not wage-weighted task exposure for 327213. I map administrative, planning, engineering analysis, quality triage, customer service, and limited machine monitoring to exposure and discount furnace, forming, maintenance, packing, and material handling; highly automated container plants may have fewer clerical tasks but more sensor-rich production than the broader group, so net bias is uncertain.
caveats: The occupational mix is 2016 and four-digit rather than current six-digit data.
The machine-learning case is from flat-glass furnace control and only an adjacent process proxy.
a.change_evidence: Current payroll and task-time data from a representative set of independent container plants, mapped to O*NET and separated among forming, quality, maintenance, logistics, engineering, sales, and administration, would materially change the estimate.
a.rationale: Broad glass manufacturing was 55.08% production occupations in 2016, with office support 8.50%, management 3.97%, architecture and engineering 2.79%, business and financial operations 2.22%, and sales 2.35%. AI can assist inspection, scheduling, furnace and line analysis, maintenance, quoting, and documentation, but most labor is coupled to continuous physical equipment and product flow.

d5.bridge: The Federal Reserve/FRED index measures domestic real production, not constant-price, constant-quality demand or the LMM customer basket. I use the six-digit output decline as the closest available demand anchor, while widening for imports, inventories, mix, post-pandemic normalization, and possible reshoring; production weakness may overstate demand decline if imports gained share.
caveats: Production is not demand and can move with imports, exports, inventories, and plant closures.
EPA's material data are from 2018 and document the installed container stream, not a current growth forecast.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year customer volume forecasts and constant-unit-equivalent shipments by beverage, food, pharmaceutical, and cosmetic end market, reconciled to imports, exports, inventory, closures, and announced capacity, would materially move the ratio.
d5.rationale: The 327213 industrial-production index fell from 108.9957 in 2022 to 80.5928 in 2025, with 2017=100, indicating severe recent domestic weakness. Glass remains important for beverages, food, pharmaceuticals, and cosmetics and benefits from inertness and recyclability, but aluminum, plastic, lightweighting, reuse systems, imports, and brand packaging shifts constrain recovery.

caveats: The injected n of 11 is an ESTIMATE derived from receipts and a sector margin rather than observed 327213 EBITDA.
Firm and establishment boundaries can differ substantially in a multi-plant manufacturing industry.
e.change_evidence: A plant-level ownership and customer map from company filings, D&B Hoovers, Capital IQ, environmental permits, and industry directories, reconciled to audited normalized EBITDA, would materially move the estimate.
e.rationale: The code closely matches a repeat external-customer model, but container furnaces and lines are capital intensive and the industry is concentrated. The injected receipts-based LMM count may include establishments inside large groups, captive operations, or firms whose true normalized EBITDA lies outside the band, reducing independent transferable eligibility.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 327213 ($1,019,963,589) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 327213 ($7,138,677,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1465 (margins.json[32]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Paper/Forest Products EBITDA/Sales 14.65% (mid-range for wood/paper/plastics)); revenue/employee $474,583 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 327213); class <5: 12 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,143,500 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $167,523 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 3 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,821,000 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $413,276 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 6 firms, avg revenue/firm $9,882,333 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,447,762 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 5 firms, avg revenue/firm $56,136,000 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $8,223,924 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 11 firms, avg revenue/firm $615,500,455 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $90,170,817 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 11

caveats: This is bounded judgment rather than a measured share.
Extensive automation can reduce staffing without eliminating the accountable operating firm under this definition.
o.change_evidence: Commercial lights-out plant benchmarks, OEM road maps for autonomous forming and inspection, regulatory quality-accountability requirements, and staffing changes after advanced-control deployments would materially move this value.
o.rationale: Any surviving demand for glass containers still requires an accountable manufacturer to melt, form, anneal, inspect, certify, and deliver a physical package. Alternative packaging reduces the quantity of glass demand and is therefore reflected in d5; it does not turn remaining glass-container demand into software or customer self-service.

caveats: No representative contract-level pass-through data was available.
Retention can differ sharply between specialized pharmaceutical or cosmetic containers and high-volume beverage bottles.
q.change_evidence: Executed supply contracts and quote histories showing term, repricing formulas, energy surcharges, mold ownership, productivity givebacks, renewal cadence, and customer concentration would materially change retention.
q.rationale: Large beverage, food, pharmaceutical, and consumer-products buyers negotiate recurrent supply agreements and can demand cost transparency or competitive rebids. Qualification, mold investment, color and quality consistency, freight economics, and reliable capacity give the producer switching friction, allowing partial but not complete retention of implemented benefits.

rho.bridge: DOE documents successful machine-learning furnace control at one large flat-glass producer rather than container plants. I use it as proof that sensor-rich glass furnaces can support real-time models, then discount for LMM capital, legacy line integration, cybersecurity, food or pharmaceutical quality systems, continuous-process risk, and change management; a sponsored large-plant case likely biases feasibility upward.
caveats: No representative 327213 implementation or realized-savings dataset was found.
This is the implementation share of exposed opportunity, not a prediction for total productivity.
rho.change_evidence: A 327213 plant survey of installed machine vision, MES/SCADA connectivity, AI pilots, validation cycles, capex, downtime, and realized labor savings, stratified by firm and furnace size, would materially move the estimate.
rho.rationale: Container production is repetitive and data-rich enough for vision inspection, furnace optimization, predictive maintenance, scheduling, and paperwork automation. Five-year implementation is nevertheless constrained by scarce downtime, validation, control-system integration, reliable labels, and the consequences of defects or line interruptions.

caveats: Neither the eligible denominator nor qualifying transfer numerator is observed.
A single transaction materially changes the measured rate when the population is this small.
s5.change_evidence: A ten-year plant ownership history from CapIQ, PitchBook, merger filings, environmental permits, and trade reports, followed by owner succession interviews and exclusion of closures, internal reorganizations, and asset-only sales, would materially change the estimate.
s5.rationale: No LMM 327213 transfer cohort was found. Qualifying control sales are limited by a very small eligible pool, long-lived integrated ownership, high replacement capex, environmental and furnace liabilities, concentrated customers, and a narrow buyer set, though succession and strategic consolidation create some five-year turnover.

principal_driver: Repetitive, sensor-rich production supports tangible AI-assisted quality and uptime improvements while every surviving unit of glass demand needs a physical manufacturer.
principal_weakness: A steep recent domestic production decline and possibly near-empty independent LMM target pool make demand and transferability the central concerns.

sources.supports: Defines the industry as establishments manufacturing glass packaging, bottling, and canning containers.
sources.title: 2022 NAICS Definition: 327213 Glass Container Manufacturing

sources.supports: Reports 85,250 jobs and broad occupation shares including production 55.08%, office support 8.50%, management 3.97%, engineering 2.79%, business and financial operations 2.22%, and sales 2.35%.
sources.title: May 2016 National Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates: NAICS 327200

sources.supports: Adjacent process evidence: a Vitro/LLNL machine-learning surrogate reduced glass-furnace analysis from as much as two weeks to minutes and was suitable for real-time control.
sources.title: High Performance Computing Improves Manufacturing Productivity and Competitiveness in the Glass Industry

sources.supports: Federal Reserve six-digit real-output proxy: 108.9957 in 2022, 98.9677 in 2023, 86.4477 in 2024, and 80.5928 in 2025, with 2017=100.
sources.title: Industrial Production: Glass Container Manufacturing (NAICS 327213)

sources.supports: Documents major container uses and reports 3.1 million tons of recycled glass containers in 2018, a 31.3% recycling rate.
sources.title: EPA Glass: Material-Specific Data

title: Glass Container Manufacturing
